CLARITY.justification: The paper is broadly well organized (intro, method, experiments, interactive study) and states its main idea—extending single-word anchors to multiword ‘tandem anchors’—early in the introduction. Notation for key matrices (Q, S, C, A) and the objective (KL reconstruction) is presented and mostly consistent, and the method and experiments sections follow logically. However, the text contains many presentation problems: repeated and garbled sentences and citations (e.g., duplicated paragraphs about related work and stochastic variational inference), awkwardly formatted Table 1 and figure references, and some incomplete/unclear transitions (references to Section 2.2 combiners without full exposition here). These issues hinder readability and require careful copy-editing and polishing to be fully clear to readers.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper clearly sits within the NLP/ML venue: it proposes a methodological extension to the anchor-word topic modeling algorithm (Sections 2–2.3) and an interactive topic-modeling workflow (Section 4), topics central to NLP/ML. The intended audience (researchers on topic models and interactive/efficient inference) is appropriate and explicitly addressed via comparisons to Arora et al. (anchor algorithm), Hu et al. (Interactive Topic Model), and Utopian. The problem formulation is standard and formalized (e.g., Eq.1 and Eq.6 for convex reconstruction using multiword ‘‘pseudowords’’), and the paper uses community-expected evaluations (20 Newsgroups dataset, classification/clustering metrics, topic coherence, and statistical tests plus a user study). Overall the positioning, baselines, and evaluation protocol align well with top-tier NLP/ML conference expectations.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important practical bottleneck—making interactive topic modeling both fast and user-friendly—by extending anchor-word methods to multiword “tandem anchors,” which (unlike sampling-based Interactive Topic Models) scale with vocabulary size and are fast enough for interactive use (Section 1, Section 4). Empirically, user-selected multiword anchors yield higher classification accuracy and improved clustering/significance metrics (Section 4.2, Figure summaries), while remaining competitive on topic coherence, showing the approach materially improves downstream utility. Because it both improves topic quality and usability and is substantially faster than prior interactive methods (comparisons to Interactive Topic Model and Utopian), this work is likely to be adopted and built upon in interactive NLP tools and opens clear follow-up directions (combiner choices, user-interface designs, broader corpora).

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a clear, concrete extension of the existing anchor-word framework: using multiword "anchor facets" and constructing synthetic pseudowords (augmenting the co-occurrence matrix Q into S with K additional rows) to run the anchor reconstruction (Sections 2.1–2.3). This is a non-trivial methodological extension of Arora et al.'s single-word anchor algorithm (Arora et al., 2012/2013) and is applied to the interactive topic-modeling setting (Section 4) with design choices (combiner functions, harmonic mean) and empirical validation. The contribution is distinct from prior interactive models (e.g., Hu et al., Utopian) and improves usability and speed, so it is notably novel though not a radical theoretical departure from existing anchor methods.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper includes a reasonable set of experiments: a “high water mark” seed experiment using 20NEWS, a user study comparing single-word vs. multiword (tandem) anchors, quantitative evaluations (classification accuracy, clustering metrics, coherence, topic significance) and statistical tests, and a combiner comparison that selects the harmonic mean. However, important details and breadth are missing for full confidence — only one main dataset is reported in detail, the user-study design, number of participants, K/topics, classifier and training details, exact combiner formulas (Section 2.2) and pseudoword construction parameters are not specified, and ablations/sensitivity (e.g., varying K, vocab thresholds, other corpora) are limited. Related work is cited and some limitations (e.g., need for metadata, coherence vs. significance tradeoff) are discussed, but failure modes and reproducibility details are insufficient to fully replicate the study.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares tandem anchors to the natural baselines (single-word anchors and unsupervised Gram‑Schmidt anchors) using multiple metrics (accuracy, clustering metrics, coherence, topic significance) and appropriate statistical tests, and it cites and discusses key prior work (Arora et al., Hu et al., Choo et al., Hoffman et al.). However, it omits direct experimental comparisons to the main interactive competitors (Interactive Topic Model, Utopian), only referencing their runtimes and reporting results from prior papers (and even notes the accuracy comparison to Hu et al. (2014) is not strictly comparable due to different train/test splits). Thus comparisons are solid within-anchor baselines but miss fair, same-condition evaluations against important interactive baselines.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear main claims (multiword "tandem" anchors improve anchor-based topic models and enable interactive use) and provides experimental evidence on 20NEWS (classification, clustering, coherence, significance metrics) and runtime comparisons (Sections 3–4). The mathematical extension (augmenting Q with K pseudoword rows and solving the same convex reconstruction, Equations 1 and 6) is plausible given prior anchor work, but the manuscript omits key technical details of pseudoword construction (Section 2.2 is referenced but not shown) and how Si,j are formed, which is central to the method. Experimental methodology has strengths (comparisons to Gram–Schmidt anchors, use of nonparametric tests when appropriate) but also gaps and inconsistencies: the text alternates between D’Agostino/t-tests with Bonferroni and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests without a clear protocol, omits important experimental details (K, classifier type, exact splits, sample sizes of interactive users), and uses LDA with fixed topics to infer token assignments without justification of potential biases. Given these omissions and methodological ambiguities, the claims are promising but not fully supported by the provided text.
